agent_output: {"executive_summary":"CQRS is architecturally justified here but the team should adopt a **pragmatic, single-database CQRS** variant — not a full event-sourced split-database topology. The core problem is a read-path mismatch: dashboards require wide, pre-aggregated data but the write model is deeply normalized across 12 tables. With only ~500 writes/day, the synchronization burden of a fully separated datastore is unjustified. The recommended approach uses materialized read models (tables/views) in the same database, updated asynchronously via domain events triggered on order writes. This eliminates expensive joins at query time, drops dashboard latency from 30-60s to sub-second, and avoids the operational complexity of a second database, eventual consistency bugs, and dual-write failure modes.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Write Model (Order Domain)","responsibility":"Handles all order creation, status updates, and courier assignments through the existing normalized 12-table schema. Emits domain events after each successful write transaction.","boundaries":"Owns the normalized schema. No dashboard queries touch these tables directly. Only the write API and event publisher operate here."},{"name":"Read Model Store (Dashboard Projections)","responsibility":"Four denormalized projection tables optimized for each dashboard query: orders_by_status_summary, delivery_success_rates, transit_time_by_zone, courier_performance. Pre-joined and pre-aggregated.","boundaries":"Same database, separate schema (e.g. `reporting`). Read-only from the application's perspective — only the projection updater writes here."},{"name":"Projection Updater","responsibility":"Subscribes to domain events (OrderCreated, OrderStatusChanged, DeliveryCompleted, CourierAssigned) and incrementally updates the four read model tables. Runs in-process or as a lightweight background worker.","boundaries":"Has read access to write model tables (for rebuilds) and write access to reporting schema. Stateless — projection state lives in the read tables."},{"name":"Dashboard Query Service","responsibility":"Serves the operations dashboard. Executes simple SELECT queries against the denormalized reporting tables. No joins required.","boundaries":"Read-only access to reporting schema. Zero access to normalized write tables."},{"name":"Projection Rebuild CLI","responsibility":"Full rebuild utility that reprocesses all 50M records to reconstruct read models from scratch. Used for schema migrations, new projections, or corruption recovery.","boundaries":"Batch process run manually or on deploy. Reads from write model, writes to reporting schema. Designed to be idempotent and resumable."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Write Model (Order Domain)","to":"Projection Updater","contract":"Domain events: {event_type, aggregate_id, timestamp, payload}. Events published via in-database transactional outbox table (outbox pattern) to guarantee at-least-once delivery.","coupling_type":"loose — event-driven, async"},{"from":"Projection Updater","to":"Read Model Store","contract":"SQL UPSERTs into denormalized projection tables. Each event handler is idempotent (using event_id deduplication).","coupling_type":"tight — direct write access, same database"},{"from":"Dashboard Query Service","to":"Read Model Store","contract":"Simple SELECT with optional WHERE/ORDER BY. No joins. Responses shaped for dashboard widgets.","coupling_type":"tight — direct read access, but read-only"},{"from":"Projection Rebuild CLI","to":"Write Model (Order Domain)","contract":"Paginated SELECT across normalized tables. Read-only. Processes in batches of 10,000.","coupling_type":"temporary — only during rebuilds"}],"data_flow":"1. API receives order mutation → writes to normalized tables → inserts event into outbox table (same transaction). 2. Projection Updater polls outbox (or uses LISTEN/NOTIFY in Postgres) → reads event → applies incremental update to relevant projection table(s) → marks event processed. 3. Dashboard Query Service reads from projection tables → returns pre-computed results in <100ms. 4. Staleness is bounded: at 500 writes/day, projection lag is typically <1 second; worst case (under load) <5 seconds.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"CQRS (Single-Database Variant)","rationale":"Separates read and write models logically without the operational burden of a second database. The problem is purely a read-model mismatch — normalized writes vs denormalized reads. A logical separation in the same DB solves this cleanly."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox","rationale":"Guarantees domain events are published if and only if the write transaction commits. Avoids dual-write problems (writing to DB + publishing to message broker in separate transactions). At 500 writes/day, polling the outbox is trivially cheap."},{"pattern":"Materialized Projections (not DB materialized views)","rationale":"Application-managed denormalized tables rather than database materialized views. This gives control over incremental updates — refreshing a materialized view over 50M rows with complex joins would itself be slow. Incremental projection updates are O(1) per event."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Event Handlers","rationale":"At-least-once delivery from the outbox means handlers must be idempotent. Using event_id deduplication ensures projections remain consistent even on replayed events."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management (Write Side)","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: order_id — contains line items, status history, delivery details, courier assignment)","Courier (root: courier_id — contains availability, performance stats)","Zone (root: zone_id — contains geographic boundaries, transit time baselines)"]},{"name":"Operations Reporting (Read Side)","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["OrderStatusSummary (projection — counts by status, updated incrementally)","DeliverySuccessRate (projection — success/failure counts by time period and zone)","TransitTimeByZone (projection — running averages using incremental mean calculation)","CourierPerformance (projection — delivery count, success rate, avg time per courier)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Operations Reporting","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Order Management publishes domain events. Operations Reporting consumes them to build projections. Order Management has no knowledge of reporting needs — clean dependency direction."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single-database CQRS instead of separate read database","gains":"No eventual consistency across databases, no replication lag management, single backup/restore, simpler ops, transactional outbox is trivial in same DB, no network partition risk between stores","sacrifices":"Read and write models share database resources (CPU, I/O, connections). Cannot independently scale read vs write database.","alternatives_considered":["Separate read database (Postgres replica or Elasticsearch) — rejected because 500 writes/day and dashboard-only reads don't justify the operational complexity","Redis cache layer — rejected because the problem is query shape (joins), not just caching; cache invalidation at this data volume is tricky and projections solve it more cleanly"]},{"decision":"Application-managed projections instead of Postgres materialized views","gains":"Incremental updates (O(1) per event vs O(n) full refresh), full control over update timing and logic, can handle complex aggregation logic","sacrifices":"More application code to maintain, projection logic must be kept in sync with domain changes, requires rebuild tooling","alternatives_considered":["Postgres REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY — rejected because refreshing over 12-table joins on 50M rows would take minutes, negating the latency goal","Postgres partial/filtered materialized views — considered but incremental maintenance is still not natively supported in Postgres"]},{"decision":"Transactional outbox over direct event publishing","gains":"Atomicity between state change and event emission, no message broker dependency, simple implementation at this write volume","sacrifices":"Polling introduces small latency (configurable, typically <1s), outbox table needs periodic cleanup","alternatives_considered":["Application-level publish after commit — rejected due to failure window where commit succeeds but publish fails","Change Data Capture (Debezium) — overkill for 500 writes/day, adds significant infrastructure"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Adding new dashboard widgets","evolution_path":"Add a new projection table and a new event handler. Existing projections are untouched. Run rebuild CLI to backfill from historical data. Zero impact on write path."},{"area":"Migrating to separate read database later","evolution_path":"If read load grows (e.g., customer-facing analytics), swap the projection updater's target from same-DB tables to an external store (Elasticsearch, ClickHouse). The outbox pattern and event handlers remain identical — only the sink changes."},{"area":"Adding real-time streaming","evolution_path":"Replace outbox polling with a proper message broker (Kafka, NATS). Event schema is already defined; projection handlers are already idempotent. Incremental migration."},{"area":"New projection shapes","evolution_path":"Each projection is independent. New business questions (e.g., 'orders by customer segment') just need a new table + handler + rebuild. No schema migration on the write side."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Event schema","reason":"Once domain events are published and consumed by projections, changing event schemas requires coordinated migration of both the publisher and all handlers. Use a versioning strategy from day one (e.g., event_type includes version: OrderStatusChanged_v1)."},{"area":"Aggregate boundaries in write model","reason":"The 12-table normalized schema is the source of truth. Changing aggregate structure (e.g., splitting Order into Order + Shipment) would require rebuilding all projections. This is manageable but not free."},{"area":"Projection consistency guarantees","reason":"The system provides eventual consistency on the read side. If future requirements demand strongly consistent reads (e.g., 'show me the order I just created, immediately'), the architecture must accommodate read-your-writes via write-model fallback queries."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Projection drift — projection logic diverges from write model semantics after domain changes","severity":"high","mitigation":"Rebuild CLI must be run as part of CI/CD pipeline for any domain model change. Automated reconciliation job compares projection totals against write model counts daily."},{"risk":"Stale dashboard data confuses operators into wrong decisions","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Display 'last updated' timestamp on every dashboard widget. At 500 writes/day with <1s projection lag, staleness is negligible — but operators should know."},{"risk":"Outbox table grows unbounded","severity":"low","mitigation":"Scheduled cleanup job deletes processed events older than 7 days. Outbox table is append-only with a processed_at index."},{"risk":"Rebuild time for 50M records is slow","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Batch processing with cursor-based pagination (10K rows/batch). Parallelizable by partition key (e.g., zone_id). Target: full rebuild in <30 minutes. Run during low-traffic windows."},{"risk":"Team over-engineers toward full event sourcing","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Document explicitly that this is CQRS without event sourcing. The write model remains a normal relational model. Events are ephemeral (outbox) not the source of truth. Clear architectural decision record."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Week 1-2): Create reporting schema with four projection tables. Build projection rebuild CLI that reads from normalized tables and populates projections. Validate dashboard queries against projections are <100ms. Phase 2 (Week 2-3): Implement transactional outbox table and domain event emission on order write paths. Build projection updater worker (polling-based, 500ms interval). Phase 3 (Week 3-4): Migrate dashboard queries from direct normalized queries to projection tables. Add reconciliation job. Add 'last updated' timestamps to dashboard. Monitor and tune.","files_to_create_or_modify":["db/migrations/001_create_reporting_schema.sql — reporting.orders_by_status_summary, reporting.delivery_success_rates, reporting.transit_time_by_zone, reporting.courier_performance","db/migrations/002_create_outbox_table.sql — domain_events_outbox (id, event_type, aggregate_id, payload JSONB, created_at, processed_at)","src/domain/events/ — OrderCreated, OrderStatusChanged, DeliveryCompleted, CourierAssigned event definitions","src/projections/handlers/ — one handler per projection, each consuming relevant events","src/projections/rebuilder.ts — full rebuild CLI with batched processing and progress reporting","src/projections/updater.ts — outbox polling worker, event dispatch to handlers","src/reporting/queries/ — simple SELECT-based query functions for each dashboard widget","src/domain/outbox.ts — outbox writer, called within write-model transaction"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each projection handler must be idempotent and testable in isolation. Use event_id for deduplication. Projection tables should have indexes optimized for the exact dashboard queries (no over-indexing). Write model changes must always emit events within the same database transaction (outbox insert in same TX). Rebuild CLI must be resumable (track last processed ID).","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT use database materialized views — they cannot be incrementally updated and refreshing over 50M rows defeats the purpose","Do NOT introduce a message broker for 500 writes/day — transactional outbox with polling is simpler and sufficient","Do NOT make projections the source of truth — they are disposable and rebuildable; the normalized write model is authoritative","Do NOT skip the rebuild CLI — it is your safety net for projection corruption, schema changes, and new projections","Do NOT add event sourcing — the write model is a normal relational model; events are a synchronization mechanism, not a persistence strategy","Do NOT query normalized tables from the dashboard 'just for one widget' — maintain the separation cleanly or the architecture erodes"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["CQRS is the right pattern for this specific read/write mismatch","Single-database variant is correct given 500 writes/day","Transactional outbox over message broker at this scale","Incremental projections over materialized view refresh","Four projection tables map cleanly to the four dashboard requirements"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact rebuild time for 50M records depends on hardware, indexes, and query plans — 30 minute estimate is approximate","Whether the existing ORM/framework supports clean outbox integration without significant refactoring","Specific index strategies for projection tables depend on exact query patterns not fully specified"],"assumptions":["Database is PostgreSQL or similar RDBMS with JSONB and LISTEN/NOTIFY support","Dashboard has four primary query patterns as described — no ad-hoc querying requirement","The team has ability to modify the write path to emit events","Sub-second dashboard response time is the target","Near-real-time (seconds, not milliseconds) projection freshness is acceptable"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"CQRS is architecturally justified here but the team should adopt a **pragmatic, single-database CQRS** variant — not a full event-sourced split-database topology. The core problem is a read-path mismatch: dashboards require wide, pre-aggregated data but the write model is deeply normalized across 12 tables. With only ~500 writes/day, the synchronization burden of a fully separated datastore is unjustified. The recommended approach uses materialized read models (tables/views

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this logistics platform reporting problem and design a CQRS solution.

Key facts:
- 50M records, highly normalized (12 tables, complex joins)
- Read queries: 30-60 seconds for dashboard (orders by status, delivery rates, avg transit time by zone, courier rankings)
- Read-only queries
- Write volume: ~500 orders/day (very low)
- Team considering CQRS

This is actually a textbook case for CQRS — or even simpler: materialized views. Let me think about the trade-offs carefully.

Dashboard Query Service reads from projection tables → returns pre-computed results in <100ms. 4. Staleness is bounded: at 500 writes/day, projection lag is typically <1 second; worst case (under load) <5 seconds.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"CQRS (Single-Database Variant)","rationale":"Separates read and write models logically without the operational burden of a second database. The problem is purely a read-model mismatch — normalized writes vs denormalized reads. A logical separation in the same DB solves this cleanly."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox","rationale":"Guarantees domain events are published if and only if the write transaction commits. Avoids dual-write problems (writing to DB + publishing to message broker in separate transactions). At 500 writes/day, polling the outbox is trivially cheap."},{"pattern":"Materialized Projections (not DB materialized views)","rationale":"Application-managed denormalized tables rather than database materialized views. This gives control over incremental updates — refreshing a materialized view over 50M rows with complex joins would itself be slow. Incremental projection updates are O(1) per event."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Event Handlers","rationale":"At-least-once delivery from the outbox means handlers must be idempotent. Using event_id deduplication ensures projections remain consistent even on replayed events."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management (Write Side)","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: order_id — contains line items, status history, delivery details, courier assignment)","Courier (root: courier_id — contains availability, performance stats)","Zone (root: zone_id — contains geographic boundaries, transit time baselines)"]},{"name":"Operations Reporting (Read Side)","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["OrderStatusSummary (projection — counts by status, updated incrementally)","DeliverySuccessRate (projection — success/failure counts by time period and zone)","TransitTimeByZone (projection — running averages using incremental mean calculation)","CourierPerformance (projection — delivery count, success rate, avg time per courier)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Operations Reporting","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Order Management publishes domain events. Operations Reporting consumes them to build projections. Order Management has no knowledge of reporting needs — clean dependency direction."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single-database CQRS instead of separate read database","gains":"No eventual consistency across databases, no replication lag management, single backup/restore, simpler ops, transactional outbox is trivial in same DB, no network partition risk between stores","sacrifices":"Read and write models share database resources (CPU, I/O, connections). Cannot independently scale read vs write database.","alternatives_considered":["Separate read database (Postgres replica or Elasticsearch) — rejected because 500 writes/day and dashboard-only reads don't justify the operational complexity","Redis cache layer — rejected because the problem is query shape (joins), not just caching; cache invalidation at this data volume is tricky and projections solve it more cleanly"]},{"decision":"Application-managed projections instead of Postgres materialized views","gains":"Incremental updates (O(1) per event vs O(n) full refresh), full control over update timing and logic, can handle complex aggregation logic","sacrifices":"More application code to maintain, projection logic must be kept in sync with domain changes, requires rebuild tooling","alternatives_considered":["Postgres REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY — rejected because refreshing over 12-table joins on 50M rows would take minutes, negating the latency goal","Postgres partial/filtered materialized views — considered but incremental maintenance is still not natively supported in Postgres"]},{"decision":"Transactional outbox over direct event publishing","gains":"Atomicity between state change and event emission, no message broker dependency, simple implementation at this write volume","sacrifices":"Polling introduces small latency (configurable, typically <1s), outbox table needs periodic cleanup","alternatives_considered":["Application-level publish after commit — rejected due to failure window where commit succeeds but publish fails","Change Data Capture (Debezium) — overkill for 500 writes/day, adds significant infrastructure"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Adding new dashboard widgets","evolution_path":"Add a new projection table and a new event handler. Rebuild CLI must be resumable (track last processed ID).","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT use database materialized views — they cannot be incrementally updated and refreshing over 50M rows defeats the purpose","Do NOT introduce a message broker for 500 writes/day — transactional outbox with polling is simpler and sufficient","Do NOT make projections the source of truth — they are disposable and rebuildable; the normalized write model is authoritative","Do NOT skip the rebuild CLI — it is your safety net for projection corruption, schema changes, and new projections","Do NOT add event sourcing — the write model is a normal relational model; events are a synchronization mechanism, not a persistence strategy","Do NOT query normalized tables from the dashboard 'just for one widget' — maintain the separation cleanly or the architecture erodes"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["CQRS is the right pattern for this specific read/write mismatch","Single-database variant is correct given 500 writes/day","Transactional outbox over message broker at this scale","Incremental projections over materialized view refresh","Four projection tables map cleanly to the four dashboard requirements"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact rebuild time for 50M records depends on hardware, indexes, and query plans — 30 minute estimate is approximate","Whether the existing ORM/framework supports clean outbox integration without significant refactoring","Specific index strategies for projection tables depend on exact query patterns not fully specified"],"assumptions":["Database is PostgreSQL or similar RDBMS with JSONB and LISTEN/NOTIFY support","Dashboard has four primary query patterns as described — no ad-hoc querying requirement","The team has ability to modify the write path to emit events","Sub-second dashboard response time is the target","Near-real-time (seconds, not milliseconds) projection freshness is acceptable"]}}
